reasoning: The informal statement "$\sum 1/k(\log(k))^p$ converges when $p > 1$" is the standard Bertrand series, which is only well-defined starting from $k = 2$ (since $\log 1 = 0$ and $k = 0$ gives division by zero). The Lean formalization encodes this by summing `fun (k : ℕ) => 1 / ((k + 2) * (Real.log (k + 2)) ^ p)` over all `k : ℕ`, which is exactly the re-indexing $k \mapsto k+2$ of $\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} 1/(n (\log n)^p)$ — the only mathematically sensible reading. The hypothesis `p > 1` matches the informal statement, and `Summable` correctly captures convergence. The shift by 2 makes explicit the implicit premise about the starting index that any mathematician would supply, so this is a faithful formalization rather than a strengthening or weakening.
